questions.question:
"India is a country of rich heritage and cultural diversity." Which one of the following facts best supports the claim made in the above sentence?

A. India is a union of 28 states and 7 union territories.
B. India has a population of over 1.1 billion.
C. India is home to 22 official languages and thousands of dialects.
D. The Indian cricket team draws players from over ten states.
questions.answer: C. India is home to 22 official languages and thousands of dialects.
questions.to_find: the fact that best supports the claim of rich heritage and cultural diversity

understand.plain: The sentence claims India has rich heritage AND cultural diversity. A supporting fact must directly evidence one or both of these attributes. Multiple official languages plus thousands of dialects is direct, tangible evidence of cultural diversity. The other options relate to size or geography but do not specifically demonstrate cultural or heritage richness.

keywords.explain: Traditions, languages, practices, and cultural artifacts passed down through generations.
keywords.example: Classical dance forms, ancient temples, and regional cuisines are part of India's heritage.

keywords.explain: Variety of distinct cultures, languages, customs, and beliefs coexisting within a single country.
keywords.example: Diwali, Eid, Christmas, and Pongal all celebrated nationally reflect cultural diversity.

keywords.explain: A piece of evidence that directly validates or strengthens the claim made in the statement.
keywords.example: To support 'India is diverse,' citing the number of languages spoken is a direct supporting fact.

understand.visual_alt: No diagram needed; this is a verbal reasoning question about identifying evidence.

given.aim: Identify which of the four options directly and most specifically supports the claim of rich heritage and cultural diversity.

terms.meaning: India is a country of rich heritage and cultural diversity.
terms.example: The sentence under analysis is the claim we must support.
terms.connects: Each option must be tested for how directly it provides evidence for this specific claim.

terms.term: Option A - 28 states and 7 union territories
terms.example: Political structure does not directly indicate cultural diversity.
terms.connects: Geographic/political diversity is not the same as cultural diversity; weakly supports the claim.

terms.term: Option C - 22 official languages and thousands of dialects
terms.meaning: India recognises 22 languages in its constitution and thousands more dialects are spoken.
terms.example: Tamil, Bengali, Telugu, Hindi, Urdu, Sanskrit all being official languages shows diversity.
terms.connects: Language diversity is a direct indicator of cultural diversity, strongly supporting the claim.

terms.meaning: India has one of the largest populations in the world.
terms.example: Large population size does not itself imply cultural or heritage richness.
terms.connects: Population size alone does not support cultural diversity.

given.plan: Evaluate each option by asking: does it directly evidence 'heritage' or 'cultural diversity'? Linguistic diversity (22 languages + thousands of dialects) is the strongest and most direct indicator of cultural diversity.

steps.title: Identify the key attributes in the claim
steps.apply: The claim has two attributes: (1) rich heritage, (2) cultural diversity. A good supporting fact should relate directly to at least one of these.
steps.note: Both attributes should ideally be addressed by the supporting fact.

steps.title: Evaluate each option
steps.apply: A: 28 states / 7 UTs - political structure, not cultural evidence. B: 1.1 billion population - size, not culture. C: 22 official languages + thousands of dialects - direct evidence of linguistic and cultural diversity. D: Cricket players from 10+ states - reflects geographic spread, not cultural/heritage richness.
steps.note: Only option C provides concrete, direct evidence of cultural diversity.

steps.title: Select the best supporting fact
steps.apply: Option C (22 official languages and thousands of dialects) directly demonstrates cultural diversity through the variety of languages, which are a primary vehicle of cultural identity and heritage.
steps.note: Languages carry culture, tradition, and heritage - making this the most direct supporting evidence.

solution.result: Option C: India is home to 22 official languages and thousands of dialects, directly supporting rich heritage and cultural diversity.

questions.formula_note: This verbal reasoning question requires logical evaluation of evidence; no mathematical formulas apply.

questions.question:
Lamenting the gradual sidelining of the arts in school curricula, a group of prominent artists wrote to the Chief Minister last year, asking him to allocate more funds to support arts education in schools. However, no such increase has been announced in this year's Budget. The artists expressed their deep anguish at many of them remaining optimistic about funding in the future.

Which of the statement(s) below is/are logically valid and can be inferred from the above statements?

i. The artists expected funding for the arts to increase this year.
ii. The Chief Minister was receptive to the idea of increasing funding for the arts.
iii. The Chief Minister is a prominent artist.
iv. Schools are giving less importance to arts education nowadays.

A. (i) and (ii)
B. (ii) and (iv)
C. (i), (ii) and (iv)
D. (i) and (iv)
questions.to_find: the logically valid inferences from the passage about arts funding

understand.plain: The passage says artists petitioned the Chief Minister for more arts funding, no increase was announced, but the artists remain optimistic. We must identify which of the four statements can be logically inferred - meaning they must follow necessarily from what is stated. We cannot infer things that go beyond the evidence in the passage.

keywords.explain: A conclusion that necessarily follows from the given premises without requiring additional assumptions.
keywords.example: If all students passed, and Ram is a student, we can infer Ram passed - this is a valid inference.

keywords.explain: Deep distress or suffering; here it reflects the artists' disappointment.
keywords.example: The artists expressed anguish because their petition was not acted upon in the Budget.

keywords.explain: Having a hopeful or positive outlook about future outcomes.
keywords.example: Despite rejection, the artists remained optimistic that funding would increase in future budgets.

understand.visual_alt: No diagram needed; this is a passage-based logical inference question.

given.aim: Determine which of the four numbered statements can be logically inferred from the passage.

terms.term: Statement (i): Artists expected funding increase this year
terms.meaning: Artists believed their petition would result in a budget increase in the current year.
terms.example: They wrote last year and expressed anguish at no increase this year - implying expectation.
terms.connects: The passage says many remain optimistic about future funding, implying they expected it this year but did not get it. However, the passage says they expressed anguish at 'many remaining optimistic' - this could mean NOT all expected it. Statement (i) cannot be fully supported.

terms.term: Statement (ii): Chief Minister was receptive to increasing arts funding
terms.meaning: The CM was open to the idea of more funding for arts.
terms.example: The passage says artists remain optimistic about future funding - this optimism can only be rational if the CM showed some receptiveness.
terms.connects: Artists' continued optimism implies CM was receptive to the idea, even though no increase was announced yet.

terms.term: Statement (iv): Schools are giving less importance to arts education nowadays
terms.meaning: Arts is being gradually sidelined in school curricula.
terms.example: The passage explicitly states 'gradual sidelining of the arts in school curricula'.
terms.connects: Directly stated in the passage, so this is a valid inference.

terms.term: Statement (iii): Chief Minister is a prominent artist
terms.example: The passage mentions 'prominent artists wrote to the Chief Minister' but does not say CM is an artist.
terms.connects: This cannot be inferred from the passage; the CM is the recipient, not a member of the artist group.

given.plan: Check each statement against the text: (i) - uncertain, (ii) - supported by artists' optimism, (iii) - not supported, (iv) - directly stated in passage. Only (ii) and (iv) are valid inferences.

steps.title: Evaluate statement (i)
steps.apply: The passage says 'many of them remaining optimistic about funding in the future' - note 'future', not 'this year'. The anguish is that despite the petition, no increase was announced. However, 'many' remain optimistic (not all), suggesting not all expected it this year. Statement (i) is not conclusively supported.
steps.note: The word 'future' and 'many' make statement (i) an overgeneralization.

steps.title: Evaluate statement (ii)
steps.apply: Artists remain optimistic about future funding. Rational optimism implies the CM was at least receptive to the idea - otherwise there would be no basis for hope. This is a valid logical inference.
steps.note: Optimism post-petition implies the CM showed some openness to the concept.

steps.title: Evaluate statements (iii) and (iv)
steps.apply: Statement (iii): The CM being an artist is not stated or implied - invalid. Statement (iv): 'Gradual sidelining of the arts in school curricula' is directly stated in the passage - valid inference.

solution.result: Statements (ii) and (iv) are logically valid inferences. Answer: B.

questions.formula_note: This verbal inference question requires passage analysis; no mathematical formulas are applicable.

questions.question:
In a world filled with uncertainty, he was glad to have many good friends. He had always assisted them in times of need and was confident that they would reciprocate. However, the events of the last week proved him wrong.

Which of the following inference(s) is/are logically valid and can be inferred from the above passage?

i. His friends were always asking him to help them.
ii. He felt that when in need of help, his friends would let him down.
iii. He was sure that his friends would help him when in need.
iv. His friends did not help him last week.

A. (i) and (iii)
B. (iii) and (iv)
C. (iii) only
D. (iv) only
questions.to_find: the logically valid inferences from the passage about friendship and reciprocation

understand.plain: The passage describes a man who helped his friends and was confident they would reciprocate (return the favor). However, last week's events proved him wrong - meaning his confidence was misplaced and his friends did NOT help him when he needed it. We must identify which numbered statements follow logically from this passage.

keywords.explain: To return a favor; to respond in kind to an action done for you.
keywords.example: He helped his friends, so he expected they would reciprocate by helping him when he needed it.

keywords.explain: Events showed that his expectation or belief was incorrect.
keywords.example: 'Proved him wrong' means his confidence that friends would help was not borne out by events.

keywords.explain: A conclusion that is necessarily true given the statements in the passage.
keywords.example: If he was confident friends would help AND events proved him wrong, then friends did NOT help - this is a valid inference.

understand.visual_alt: No diagram needed; this is a passage-based logical inference question.

given.aim: Determine which of statements (i)-(iv) logically follow from the passage.

terms.term: Statement (iii): He was sure friends would help him when in need
terms.meaning: He was confident his friends would reciprocate his help.
terms.example: Passage says he 'was confident that they would reciprocate' - direct support.
terms.connects: This is explicitly stated in the passage - 'confident that they would reciprocate'.

terms.term: Statement (iv): His friends did not help him last week
terms.meaning: Friends failed to assist him in the recent time of need.
terms.example: 'Events of the last week proved him wrong' means his confidence in reciprocation was wrong - so they did not help.
terms.connects: He was confident they would reciprocate; being proved wrong means they did not - directly deducible.

terms.term: Statement (i): Friends were always asking him to help them
terms.example: Passage says he 'always assisted them in times of need' but does not say they were always asking.
terms.connects: Cannot be inferred - he may have helped proactively without being asked.

terms.term: Statement (ii): He felt friends would let him down
terms.meaning: He anticipated being disappointed by friends.
terms.example: The passage says he was 'confident' they would help - the opposite of expecting to be let down.
terms.connects: This contradicts the passage; he expected help, not disappointment.

given.plan: Statement (iii) is directly stated ('confident they would reciprocate'). Statement (iv) follows by negation: confident of help + proved wrong = friends did not help. Statements (i) and (ii) cannot be validly inferred.

steps.title: Map the passage to logical propositions
steps.apply: P1: He always helped friends in need. P2: He was confident friends would reciprocate (help him when needed). P3: Events of last week proved him wrong.
steps.note: These three premises form the logical basis for evaluating each statement.

steps.title: Evaluate statement (iii)
steps.apply: P2 directly states he was confident friends would help him. This is exactly what statement (iii) says. Valid inference.
steps.note: Directly supported by the word 'confident' in the passage.

steps.title: Evaluate statement (iv)
steps.apply: P2 says he expected friends to help. P3 says he was proved wrong. By modus tollens: if his confidence about help was proven wrong, then friends did NOT help him last week. Valid inference.
steps.note: 'Proved him wrong' means the reciprocation he expected did not occur.

steps.title: Eliminate statements (i) and (ii)
steps.apply: Statement (i) - passage says he helped them, not that they always asked. Not inferable. Statement (ii) - he felt confident, not doubtful. This is the opposite of what is stated. Both are invalid.
steps.note: Only (iii) and (iv) can be logically derived from the passage.

solution.result: Statements (iii) and (iv) are the valid inferences. Answer: B.

questions.formula_note: This verbal reasoning question involves logical inference from a passage; no mathematical formulas are used.

questions.question:
Leela is older than her cousin Pavithra. Pavithra's brother Shiva is older than Leela. When Pavithra and Shiva are visiting Leela, all three like to play chess. Pavithra wins more often than Leela does.

Which one of the following statements must be TRUE based on the above?

A. When Shiva plays chess with Leela and Pavithra, he often loses.
B. Leela is the oldest of the three.
C. Shiva is a better chess player than Pavithra.
D. Pavithra is a better chess player than Leela.
questions.answer: D. Pavithra is a better chess player than Leela.
questions.to_find: which statement must be TRUE based on the given facts about age and chess performance

understand.plain: We are given facts about the ages of three people (Leela, Pavithra, Shiva) and about their chess-playing outcomes. The key distinction is that age and chess skill are independent - being older does not mean being a better chess player. We must find the statement that MUST be true given all the facts, not just possibly true.

keywords.explain: A statement that is necessarily true given all the conditions - it cannot be false under any valid interpretation.
keywords.example: If Pavithra wins more often than Leela, then it MUST be true that Pavithra is a better chess player than Leela.

keywords.explain: Relative age ordering among the three people.
keywords.example: Shiva > Leela > Pavithra in age, based on the given relationships.

keywords.explain: A direct measure of relative chess skill between two players.
keywords.example: Pavithra winning more often than Leela directly implies Pavithra plays chess better than Leela.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 480 200' width='480' height='200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='480' height='200' rx='12' class='bg'/><text x='240' y='28' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Age Order (oldest to youngest)</text><rect x='30' y='50' width='120' height='50' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='90' y='78' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Shiva</text><text x='90' y='94' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>oldest</text><rect x='180' y='50' width='120' height='50' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='240' y='78' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Leela</text><text x='240' y='94' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>middle</text><rect x='330' y='50' width='120' height='50' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='390' y='78' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Pavithra</text><text x='390' y='94' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>youngest</text><line x1='150' y1='75' x2='180' y2='75' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><line x1='300' y1='75' x2='330' y2='75' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><text x='90' y='160' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Chess: Pavithra wins</text><text x='90' y='178' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>more than Leela</text><rect x='180' y='145' width='120' height='40' rx='6' fill='#fff5e0' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2'/><text x='240' y='160' text-anchor='middle' class='tag no'>Age != Chess Skill</text><text x='240' y='178' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Independent facts</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Age order from oldest to youngest is Shiva, Leela, Pavithra; chess skill is a separate independent fact.

given.aim: Identify which of the four statements MUST be true given all the provided information.

terms.meaning: Shiva is oldest, Leela is in the middle, Pavithra is youngest.
terms.example: Given: Leela > Pavithra (age), and Shiva > Leela (age), so Shiva > Leela > Pavithra.
terms.connects: Age order helps eliminate wrong options about who is oldest.

terms.meaning: Pavithra wins more chess games than Leela does.
terms.example: In their chess games together, Pavithra is the more frequent winner against Leela.
terms.connects: This directly establishes Pavithra as a stronger chess player than Leela.

terms.meaning: Chess skill is NOT determined by age; they are independent attributes.
terms.example: A younger person can be a better chess player than an older person.
terms.connects: We cannot use age order to infer chess skill order - this eliminates options that assume older means better at chess.

terms.meaning: No information about how often Shiva wins is given in the passage.
terms.example: The passage only tells us Pavithra wins more than Leela; Shiva's chess results are unknown.
terms.connects: Options about Shiva's chess performance (A and C) cannot be concluded.

given.plan: Derive the age order: Shiva > Leela > Pavithra. Note chess results only state Pavithra wins more than Leela. Check each option: A - Shiva losing often is not stated. B - Leela oldest is wrong (Shiva is oldest). C - Shiva vs Pavithra chess unknown. D - Pavithra wins more than Leela directly means Pavithra is better.

steps.title: Establish age order
steps.apply: Given: Leela is older than Pavithra (Leela > Pavithra). Pavithra's brother Shiva is older than Leela (Shiva > Leela). Combined: Shiva > Leela > Pavithra.
steps.note: Pavithra is the youngest; Leela is middle; Shiva is oldest.

steps.title: Evaluate option B
steps.apply: Option B says Leela is the oldest. But Shiva > Leela, so Leela is NOT the oldest. Option B is FALSE.
steps.note: Eliminated by the age order derivation.

steps.title: Evaluate options A and C
steps.apply: Option A: Shiva often loses chess. The passage gives no information about Shiva's chess performance. Cannot be concluded. Option C: Shiva is a better chess player than Pavithra. Again, no information about Shiva's chess results is given. Cannot be concluded.
steps.note: Both A and C require assumptions not supported by the passage.

steps.title: Evaluate option D
steps.apply: Option D: Pavithra is a better chess player than Leela. The passage directly states 'Pavithra wins more often than Leela does.' Winning more often IS the definition of being a better player. This MUST be true.
steps.note: This is directly derivable from the stated chess win frequency.

solution.result: Option D must be TRUE: Pavithra is a better chess player than Leela, as directly evidenced by Pavithra winning more often.

questions.formula_note: This logical reasoning question requires deduction from given facts; no mathematical formulas apply.
